volumeTitle: Amphitryon. The Comedy of Asses. The Pot of Gold. The Two Bacchises. The Captives
label: The Two Bacchises (Section 5.2.1201 - Section 5.2.1210)

sections.label: Section 5.2.1201
sections.original: tua sum ópera et propter te ímprobior .
sections.translation: Through your doing , and for your sake am I corrupted .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1201b
sections.translation: I certainly had rather ’ twere for your own than for mine .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1202b
sections.translation: What I have once said , I will not alter .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1203
sections.translation: The day wears apace . Come into the house , to take your places at table : your sons are expecting within —

sections.label: Section 5.2.1204b
sections.translation: How soon , in fact , we may be dead , d ’ ye mean ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.1205
sections.translation: ’ Tis evening , already ; come , follow us .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1205b
sections.original: Dúcite nos quo lúbet tamquam quidem addíctos .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1206
sections.original: Lepide ípsi hi sunt captí , suis qui filiís fecere insídias .
sections.translation: Right cleverly are these persons entrapped [*] themselves , who for their sons had laid a snare .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1207
sections.original: Hí senes nisi fuíssent nihili iam índe ab adulescéntia ,
sections.translation: Had not these old men been worthless from their youth upwards , they would not , with their hoary heads , have this day done an action so disgraceful ; nor , indeed , should we have represented this , if we had not , before this , seen it happen

sections.label: Section 5.2.1210
sections.original: ut apud lenones rivales filiis fierent patres .
sections.translation: that fathers became the rivals of their sons in the houses of procurers . Spectators , we wish you Farewell ! and that you will grant us loud applause .
